mechanism: Participants perform repeated grasping actions in a virtual environment on food and control objects. 3D kinematic trajectories of hand movements are recorded and segmented by object type. Latency and speed metrics are extracted per grasp. The difference in collection speed between food and non-food objects is computed; this difference is then correlated with individual-level continuous covariates (body mass index, diet attitudes) using Pearson or Spearman correlation. No formal hypothesis test is reported; the result is descriptive.
domain: embodied cognition, eating behavior, virtual reality

mechanism: Two samples of subjects (less-frequent and frequent social media users) are exposed to social media cues and control cues in randomized order. Spontaneous affective reactions are measured using the Affect Misattribution Procedure, which infers underlying affect from a response to an ambiguous target. For each participant and condition, a reaction score is computed. Differences between groups and cues are tested using analysis of variance or between-group t-tests. In Study 2, mediation analysis is conducted: the cue-type effect on craving is regressed through the affective reaction measure using path models.
domain: social media behavior, affective response, implicit cognition

mechanism: In two studies, subjects are presented with narratives or prompts and asked to engage in imaginative activity. A binary response (resistance or acceptance) is recorded per prompt. Frequencies and proportions of resistance are tabulated per genre or condition. Statistical tests (chi-square or Fisher exact) compare resistance rates across levels of a categorical predictor (genre). If sample sizes permit, logistic regression estimates the effect of genre on log-odds of resistance.
domain: philosophy, aesthetics, imagination, experimental philosophy

mechanism: Subjects judge whether a behavior described in a scenario counts as intentional. For each scenario, response frequencies or proportions are recorded. Contingency tables cross scenario type (varying side-effect presence or consequence type) against response distribution. Chi-square or proportional tests assess association between scenario attributes and intentionality judgment. The report focuses on describing empirical patterns in ordinary-language use.
domain: philosophy of action, semantics, experimental philosophy

mechanism: Respondents report participation in household tasks across 32 countries (binary or ordinal for each task). Mokken scaling is applied: tasks are ranked by difficulty or rarity to construct a unidimensional latent scale. Guttman-like response patterns identify whether respondents fit the hierarchy monotonically. A typology is constructed from scale scores. Multilevel multinomial logistic regression models the log-odds of scale score level as a function of individual-level predictors and country random intercepts, with country-level interactions for cross-national heterogeneity.
domain: sociology, gender, household labor, comparative cross-national

mechanism: Pooled cross-sectional survey data from multiple waves (2004-2013) and multiple countries are aggregated. For each country-year-education combination, mean or proportion estimates of four EU-framing dimensions are computed from discrete Likert or binary items. Time series of these means are analyzed per country; country-level macroeconomic aggregates (GDP growth, unemployment) are matched to survey dates. Regression models (linear or logistic by outcome type) relate the framing measures to economic indicators and country factors, with country and year fixed effects or random intercepts.
domain: political sociology, European public opinion, economic crisis, survey research

mechanism: Individual-level data from 25 countries are harmonized. For each respondent, the outcome is timing and type of first union (categorical: married, cohabiting, or censored). Event-history or logistic models estimate the effect of parental SES (typically ordinal or linear index) on union-type transition rates or log-odds, adjusting for respondent education. A two-step meta-analytical approach pools country-specific estimates: first-stage within-country regressions; second-stage meta-regression of country slopes on country-level characteristics (e.g., demographic transition score) to explain cross-national variation.
domain: demography, family sociology, socioeconomic inequality, comparative cross-national

mechanism: Younger and older adult subjects complete a hypothetical donation task: for each recipient type (relative vs. nonrelative) and social distance (close vs. distant), the subject specifies a donation amount (money or time). The donation matrix is analyzed using linear regression or ANOVA: donation amount is regressed on age group, recipient category, social distance, and their interactions. If the data support a kinship-selection or social-discounting model (a Gaussian or exponential decay), nonlinear curve fitting or mediation analysis may be applied to estimate discounting parameters.
domain: gerontology, behavioral economics, prosocial behavior, life-span development

mechanism: Newly diagnosed diabetes patients are randomized to intervention (5-step self-management course) or control. Outcome measures include proactive coping, goal attainment, and self-efficacy, assessed via questionnaires at baseline and follow-up. For each outcome, pre-post change is computed per group; between-group differences in change are tested using independent-samples t-tests or ANCOVA, adjusting for baseline scores if reported. Course evaluation feedback is summarized descriptively.
domain: health education, diabetes self-management, behavioral intervention, chronic disease

mechanism: Subjects view stimuli with either a single product unit or multiple identical product replicates. Efficacy perception is measured via a Likert-scale questionnaire item. Between the single-unit and replicate conditions, efficacy means are compared using an independent-samples t-test or analysis of variance. Mediation analysis is conducted: perceived entitativity (homogeneity/unity) is regressed on replication condition; efficacy is regressed on both condition and entitativity. Indirect effects are tested using bootstrap resampling or product-of-coefficients approach.
domain: consumer psychology, product perception, behavioral economics

mechanism: Subjects are placed in conditions manipulating goal conflict (via scenario or priming). Time allocation to work and leisure activities is measured or recorded. A between-groups comparison tests whether goal-conflict condition differs from control in time spent on work (or leisure) using an independent-samples t-test. Mediational pathways are tested: goal conflict→justification salience→time allocation, estimated via path regression or bootstrap mediation analysis. Process is validated across multiple operationalizations of goal conflict and work/leisure domains.
domain: consumer psychology, decision-making, time allocation, behavioral economics

mechanism: Subjects are exposed to a financial constraint manipulation (text prime or scenario). Preference is measured as a binary or continuous choice/rating between material goods and experiential purchases. Between-group comparisons use chi-square (for discrete choice) or t-test (for continuous preference). Mediation analysis tests whether the effect of financial constraint on good-vs-experience preference is mediated by longevity concern: constraint→longevity concern→preference, estimated via path regression or bootstrap. Downstream outcomes (willingness-to-pay, quantity choice) are regressed on saturation or constraint condition.
domain: consumer psychology, decision-making, financial constraint, behavioral economics

mechanism: Subjects are exposed to economic-condition scenarios (recession vs. control). In response, they allocate resources (e.g., bequest, gifts, activity assignment) to targets differing in sex and age. Allocation amounts or choices are analyzed using univariate tests (t-test or ANOVA) or logistic regression to compare economic condition effects. Mediation analysis tests whether economic condition affects child-sex preference through a latent mediator (perceived reproductive value or expected offspring count), estimated via path regression. Moderation effects (e.g., by risk aversion or age) are tested via interaction terms.
domain: consumer psychology, family economics, decision-making, evolutionary perspective

mechanism: Subjects view brand logos with circular or angular shapes and then rate product attributes on Likert scales. Attribute ratings are compared between logo shapes using t-tests or ANOVA. Mediation analysis tests whether logo shape→(softness/hardness associations)→attribute judgments using path regression. Imagery generation is manipulated (visual load task, individual-difference dispositions, ad headline clarity); attribute rating effects are compared across these constraint conditions, with interaction tests assessing whether the shape effect diminishes when the working memory imagery component is engaged or unavailable.
domain: marketing, brand perception, consumer psychology, visual cognition

mechanism: Subjects are primed with resource scarcity or abundance. Subsequently, in an unrelated product-use task, subjects generate use ideas or solutions; the number and novelty of generated uses are scored (by count and rater judgment). Between-condition means are compared using t-tests or nonparametric tests. Functional fixedness (rated or measured as tendency to use products for canonical purposes) is tested as a mediator: scarcity→reduced fixedness→increased novelty, estimated via path regression. Context-independence is demonstrated by varying the subsequent task domain across studies.
domain: consumer psychology, creativity, resource scarcity, decision-making
structure: other: text coding or narrative analysis

mechanism: Subjects view arrays of healthy and unhealthy food items arranged with lateral positioning (healthy left vs. right). Choice (binary or ranked) and consumption amount of each item type are recorded. Between-group comparisons use chi-square (choice) or t-test (consumption volume). Mediation analysis tests whether lateral arrangement→ease of processing→self-control→choice, estimated via path regression. Process measures (self-reported processing ease, choice justification) are collected and incorporated into mediation models.
domain: consumer psychology, food choice, health behavior, nudging

mechanism: Subjects are exposed to limited-quantity promotion advertising (scarcity condition) or control advertising. Subsequently, in a task involving procurement or competitive access to a good, aggressive behavior is measured via multiple operationalizations: direct harm to a competitor (e.g., loud noise intensity), willingness-to-harm ratings, or response latency/accuracy under threat. Between-group comparisons use t-tests or ANOVA. Mediation analysis tests whether exposure→(perceived competitiveness or physiological arousal)→aggressive behavior, estimated via path regression and physiological measurement (e.g., cortisol, skin conductance).
domain: consumer psychology, behavioral economics, emotion, competitive behavior

mechanism: Subjects view products (objects or product images) with color saturation manipulated across conditions. Perceived size is measured via direct rating or indirect measurement (e.g., comparison to reference object). Between-condition means are compared using t-tests or ANOVA. Mediation analysis tests whether saturation→attentional capture→arousal→size perception, using path regression and measuring attention via eye-tracking or reaction time, arousal via self-report or physiological signal. Downstream effects (preference, willingness-to-pay, quantity choice) are regressed on saturation and its interaction with product usage goals.
domain: marketing, sensory perception, consumer psychology, visual design

mechanism: Subjects view consumer products with user-rating distributions that are either unimodal or bimodal (polarizing). Product preference is measured via Likert rating or choice. Between-distribution-type means are compared using t-tests or ANOVA. Mediation analysis tests whether bimodal distribution→(increased perceived self-expressiveness)→increased preference, estimated via path regression. Moderation by self-concept clarity (state or trait) and by relevance of the rating dimension to self-expression are tested via interaction terms and moderation analysis.
domain: consumer psychology, self-concept, identity, behavioral economics

mechanism: Respondents complete surveys measuring financial well-being (two dimensions: current money-management stress and expected future financial security), overall well-being, and confounding life-domain measures (job satisfaction, health, relationship support). Within-subject narratives and large-scale survey data are used to conceptualize and measure the constructs. Linear regression models overall well-being on the two financial well-being dimensions plus controls for other life domains. Domain-level effect sizes are compared to assess relative importance. Subgroup analysis (by income group) tests for heterogeneity in relationships. Mediation and differential-antecedent analyses examine how the two financial dimensions are determined.
domain: consumer psychology, financial well-being, quality of life, behavioral economics
